title: Leveraging LLM Agents and Digital Twins for Fault Handling in Process Plants

agents.description: Observes the current state of the physical plant using sensor data, performance monitors, alarm indicators, and diagnostic thresholds. Identifies potential fault symptoms based on deviations from nominal behavior.

Implemented as PlantOperatorCrew. Leverages an LLM-driven approach to synthesize corrective control actions based on the current system state. Consults plant-specific information from the Digital Process Plant Twin, previous interactions, and alternative actions to generate potential corrective control actions.

Implemented as validation_script.py. Assesses the feasibility, safety, and overall effectiveness of the corrective control actions proposed by the Action Agent after simulation. Ensures that any action proposed for real-world deployment adheres strictly to operational protocols and safety standards.

Implemented as PlantStrategyCrew. Intervenes when initial corrective control actions fail validation. Iteratively refines the corrective strategy by incorporating feedback from the Simulation until a valid solution is identified.

orchestration.orchestration_pattern_description: The framework uses a closed-loop multi-agent orchestration pattern. A Monitoring Agent detects faults and triggers the Action Agent to generate corrective actions. These are tested in a Digital Twin Simulation, then evaluated by the Validation Agent. If invalid, the Reprompting Agent iteratively refines the actions. The loop continues until a valid corrective action is found or a maximum iteration count is reached, at which point safety systems are triggered. The orchestration is coordinated by a main.py script using CrewAI and a RouterFlow.

multi_agent_coordination_strategy.multi_agent_coordination_strategy_descri: Agents cooperate in a closed-loop feedback structure: Monitoring Agent detects faults, Action Agent proposes corrective actions, Validation Agent checks safety and feasibility, and Reprompting Agent refines actions when needed. A central orchestration script (main.py) coordinates the iterative interaction between agents, the Digital Twin simulation, and the physical plant.

human_integration.human_integration_description: The framework is designed to progressively reduce human intervention in fault handling. Human operators are replaced by autonomous agents for routine fault detection and corrective action. However, a Safety System acts as a fallback when no valid corrective action can be found after a defined number of iterations, triggering predefined emergency protocols including shutdown procedures or manual intervention. Chain-of-thought prompting ensures transparency and traceability for human oversight.

retrieval.application_domain_description: Autonomous fault handling in industrial process plants, specifically controlling and correcting faults (e.g., pipe clogging) in a modular mixing module of a process plant using LLM agents and a Digital Twin.
retrieval.problem_description: Fault handling in process plants is a cognitively demanding, predominantly manual process that relies heavily on human expertise. The same observable symptom may stem from multiple root causes, each requiring a different response. This ambiguity is rarely captured in predefined operator instructions, making human expertise indispensable. Combined with increasing plant complexity and a shortage of experienced operators, there is an urgent need for more autonomous fault handling solutions. LLMs lack plant-specific knowledge and fault handling requires sequential reasoning steps that need structured orchestration.
retrieval.proposed_solution: A methodological framework integrating LLM-based agents with a Digital Process Plant Twin. The framework distributes fault handling responsibilities across four agents: Monitoring Agent, Action Agent, Validation Agent, and Reprompting Agent. The Digital Twin serves both as a structured repository of plant-specific engineering knowledge for agent prompting and as a simulation platform for validation and verification of corrective actions. A tailored prompt engineering strategy based on systems engineering principles (structure, function, behavior) is used to supply agents with plant-specific knowledge.
retrieval.research_methodology_description: The framework is evaluated using a simulated mixing module from a benchmark process plant implemented in OpenModelica. Different prompt formats (natural language text, Modelica code, State Machine + P&ID) and two LLM models (GPT-4o and GPT-4o-mini) are compared across metrics including action quality, reprompts needed, and token usage for a pipe clogging fault scenario.

decision_quality_improvement: GPT-4o with Text input achieved perfect control performance (15/15 correct actions) with only 1 reprompt.
GPT-4o with SM+P&ID format achieved 14/15 correct actions with 5 reprompts.
GPT-4o-mini with SM+P&ID achieved 13/15 correct actions with 9 reprompts.
GPT-4o-mini with Text achieved 13/15 correct actions with 6 reprompts.
Structuring prompts according to system engineering principles (structure, function, behavior) contributed to generation of appropriate corrective actions.
response_time_impact: Fewer reprompts indicate faster decision-making; Text format for GPT-4o required only 1 reprompt indicating excellent loop convergence.
Modelica Code format required the highest number of reprompts (6 for GPT-4o, 10 for GPT-4o-mini), indicating slower convergence.
Latency in closed-loop interactions between LLM agents and simulation is identified as a concern for future work.
reliability_metrics: GPT-4o Text: 15/15 correct actions, 0 incorrect actions, 1 reprompt.
GPT-4o Modelica Code: 12/15 correct actions, 3 missed pump actions, 6 reprompts.
GPT-4o SM+P&ID: 14/15 correct actions, 1 missed pump action, 5 reprompts.
GPT-4o-mini Text: 13/15 correct actions, 2 missed valve actions, 6 reprompts.
GPT-4o-mini Modelica Code: 12/15 correct actions, 2 incorrect valve actions, 3 missed pump actions, 10 reprompts.
GPT-4o-mini SM+P&ID: 13/15 correct actions, 1 incorrect valve action, 2 missed valve actions, 9 reprompts.

ce_technique_effectiveness.chain-of-thought: Enables transparent and traceable decision-making; supports post-hoc interpretation, validation, and human oversight.
ce_technique_effectiveness.in-context-learning: Text-based plant description (natural language) yielded best performance for both GPT-4o (15/15) and GPT-4o-mini (13/15); structured engineering artifacts (SM+P&ID) also performed well.
ce_technique_effectiveness.iterative-reasoning: Closed-loop reprompting with simulation feedback enabled iterative refinement of corrective actions, achieving valid solutions in most cases with few reprompts.

failure_modes: Missed pump actions when using Modelica Code format, indicating difficulty interpreting behavioral logic.
Incorrect valve actions observed with GPT-4o-mini on Modelica Code and SM+P&ID formats.
Higher reprompt counts with Modelica Code format suggest challenges in interpreting embedded control logic.
limitations: Study focused on batch production processes; continuous-time systems exhibit more complex dynamics requiring additional domain knowledge and more reprompts.
Limited context window of current LLMs restricts how much structured content can be processed per iteration.
RAG may mitigate context window limitations but introduces additional latency.
Experimental setup only considered a single module; multi-module setups with more actuators and sensors will increase fault handling difficulty.
Modelica-specific training data may be underrepresented in LLM training datasets, limiting performance with Modelica Code format.
Token usage varies substantially across representations; Modelica Code leads to highest token consumption (up to 113K).

identified_research_gaps: Extension to continuous-time systems with more complex dynamics requiring additional domain knowledge and longer latencies
Addressing limited context window of current LLMs restricting structured content processing per iteration
Integration of Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) to enable real-time access to structured plant data or documentation
Incorporating more expressive behavioral models such as differential equation-based descriptions for continuous dynamics
Complementary sub-symbolic AI methods such as ML-based anomaly detection to strengthen the Monitoring Agent
Reducing iteration latency in closed-loop interactions through parallelized simulations, targeted state updates, and surrogate or reduced-order models
Scaling to more complex multi-module setups with greater numbers of actuators and sensors
